tactics.name: Power Pause
tactics.description: A deliberate silence of 2–5 seconds placed after a key statement or question to let it land fully and signal confidence.
tactics.scoringHints: Look for speakers who pause deliberately mid-sentence or after a key point. Not nervous pausing — intentional stillness with maintained eye contact. The speaker doesn't fill the silence with 'um' or 'so'.

tactics.name: Vocal Crescendo
tactics.description: Gradually increasing volume, pace, and intensity over the course of a sentence or passage, building to a climactic peak.
tactics.scoringHints: Starting quiet and measured, then building to full voice and pace. The escalation should feel earned — not sudden. Common in motivational speeches and stage monologues.

tactics.name: Strategic Whisper
tactics.description: Dropping volume to near-whisper at a key moment to force the audience to lean in and focus — creates intimacy and urgency.
tactics.scoringHints: The whisper must be intentional and contrasting — not timid. The speaker's posture stays open and confident while the voice drops. Effective speakers often pair whisper with a leaning-in movement.

tactics.name: Vocal Fry Control
tactics.description: Intentionally eliminating or strategically using vocal fry (the low, crackling tone produced at the bottom of the register).
tactics.scoringHints: Listen for the raspy, low-frequency crackle at the end of sentences. In coaching, the goal is usually elimination — but note that some speakers use it intentionally for a casual, intimate effect.

tactics.name: Chest Voice Anchor
tactics.description: Grounding vocal delivery in the chest resonator to produce a fuller, warmer, more authoritative tone versus head or throat voice.
searchKeywords: chest voice vs head voice speaking
how to speak from your chest
tactics.scoringHints: A chest-anchored voice has warmth, fullness, and carries without effort. Place hand on sternum — a well-anchored voice vibrates there. Compare to throat voice which sounds thin and strained.

tactics.name: Pitch Drop
tactics.description: Deliberately lowering pitch at the end of a statement — as opposed to upspeak (rising pitch) — to signal authority and certainty.
tactics.scoringHints: The final word or syllable of a confident statement should fall in pitch. Upspeak (rising at the end) turns statements into questions. Pitch drop turns observations into declarations.

tactics.name: Staccato Delivery
tactics.description: Separating words or short phrases with micro-pauses, giving each unit individual impact — like bullet points spoken aloud.
tactics.scoringHints: Each phrase or key word gets its own space — not rushed into the next. Often used by coaches and military speakers. The rhythm is: speak. pause. speak. pause. — creating a drumbeat effect.

tactics.name: Elongation
tactics.description: Stretching the vowel of a key word to add emotional weight and draw the listener's focus to that specific word.
tactics.scoringHints: The elongated word takes twice as long as surrounding words. Common in politicians and preachers ('We shall ooooovercome'). Powerful when used sparingly — loses effect if overused.

tactics.name: Breath Pacing
tactics.description: Using deliberate breath patterns to control speaking rhythm and pace — breathing at phrase breaks rather than gasping mid-sentence.
tactics.scoringHints: Well-paced breathing is invisible — breaths happen at phrase breaks and don't interrupt the message. Poor breath control shows as mid-sentence gasps, trailing volume, or rushing to finish before running out of air.

tactics.name: Rhythmic Cadence
tactics.description: Speaking with a musical, poetic rhythm — using parallelism and repeated sentence structure to create a hypnotic pattern that keeps listeners engaged.
tactics.scoringHints: MLK's 'I have a dream' is the iconic example. The rhythm repeats (anaphora), the sentences are parallel, and the beat is almost musical. Look for sentences that could be spoken to a drum track.

tactics.name: Pacing and Leading
tactics.description: First matching the other person's communication style, pace, and energy (pacing), then gradually shifting your own state to guide them toward a different one (leading).
tactics.scoringHints: Watch for a speaker who initially matches the other person's tone and pace, then gradually shifts — and the other person follows. The transition is subtle. If done well, the listener doesn't notice the redirect.

tactics.name: Reframing
tactics.description: Changing the context or perspective through which a statement is interpreted, transforming a negative into a neutral or positive without denying the original fact.
tactics.scoringHints: A reframe acknowledges the original statement and then offers a new interpretation: 'That's not a problem — that's an opportunity to...' Watch for language that accepts the premise but shifts the conclusion.

tactics.name: Socratic Questioning
tactics.description: Guiding someone to a conclusion through a carefully sequenced series of questions rather than declarative statements.
tactics.scoringHints: The speaker never tells — they ask. Each question accepts the other person's answer and builds the next question from it. The listener reaches the conclusion believing it was their own idea. Common in therapy and coaching.

tactics.name: Elicitation
tactics.description: Drawing out information naturally through conversation rather than direct questioning — making the other person volunteer information they didn't plan to share.
tactics.scoringHints: The speaker makes statements that invite corrections or elaborations rather than asking direct questions. 'I heard that company was struggling...' invites the other person to correct/expand without feeling interrogated.

tactics.name: Strategic Vulnerability
tactics.description: Deliberately disclosing a personal struggle, mistake, or uncertainty to build trust and lower the other person's defences.
tactics.scoringHints: The disclosure is genuine but controlled — revealing something real without overwhelming the listener. It signals 'I'm human' and 'I trust you enough to share this.' Watch for how quickly rapport shifts after the disclosure.

tactics.name: Future Pacing
tactics.description: Describing a desired future state so vividly that the listener can already see themselves there — making the outcome feel inevitable rather than aspirational.
tactics.scoringHints: The speaker uses present tense for future events ('When you have this...', 'As you step into that meeting...'). The description is sensory-rich. The listener nods along because they're already imagining it.

tactics.name: Presupposition
tactics.description: Embedding an assumption in a statement so that accepting the statement requires accepting the assumption — bypassing conscious resistance.
tactics.scoringHints: 'Which would you prefer — Tuesday or Thursday?' presupposes you're meeting. 'How soon do you want to get started?' presupposes you will start. Look for questions that only make sense if you accept the embedded premise.

tactics.name: Benjamin Franklin Effect
tactics.description: Asking someone for a small, reasonable favour first — which makes them more favourably disposed toward you when you make a subsequent larger request.
tactics.scoringHints: The small initial ask must be genuine and low-cost for the other person. Watch for the shift in tone — after granting a small request, people tend to be more open to the larger conversation.

tactics.name: Door in the Face
tactics.description: Opening with a large, likely-rejected request, then following with a smaller, real request — which gets accepted because of the contrast with what was refused.
searchKeywords: door in the face technique negotiation
tactics.scoringHints: The initial large ask must be real — not obviously absurd. The subsequent ask must seem genuinely smaller by comparison. Watch for the 'reasonable' tone shift after the first refusal.

tactics.name: Embedded Commands
tactics.description: Hiding directive language inside a larger, innocent-sounding sentence — delivering the instruction at the unconscious level.
tactics.scoringHints: 'Some people find that when they just relax and take action, things work out.' The embedded command is 'relax and take action.' Watch for slight vocal emphasis on the embedded phrase — speakers who know this technique often mark it with a tonal shift.

tactics.name: Triangle Gaze
tactics.description: Moving eye contact in a triangular pattern between the other person's two eyes and their mouth — covering the face without staring fixedly at one point.
searchKeywords: where to look when making eye contact
tactics.scoringHints: Social triangle: eyes → nose → mouth. Professional triangle (business): eyes → left eye → right eye → nose. The movement is slow and natural — not darting. Watch for smooth scanning versus fixed staring.

tactics.name: Open Palm Display
tactics.description: Showing the palms during gestures or when making statements — a primal signal of honesty and non-threat that increases perceived trustworthiness.
tactics.scoringHints: Palms up during speech signal openness and invitation. Palms down signal authority and finality. Palms hidden (in pockets, clasped) signal hidden information or anxiety. Watch for which display correlates with which message.

tactics.name: Chin Tuck Authority
tactics.description: A slight tucking of the chin (not down, but slightly in) when delivering authoritative statements — projects a grounded, serious tone and avoids the vulnerable chin-up or submissive chin-down.
tactics.scoringHints: The chin tuck keeps the neck vertical and the gaze level — not raised (arrogant) or lowered (submissive). Politicians and negotiators often adopt this unconsciously. Compare different chin positions on the same speaker.

tactics.name: Territorial Stance
tactics.description: Occupying space deliberately and fully — feet wide, arms away from body, possessions spread — to claim the environment as your own and signal comfortable authority.
tactics.scoringHints: Watch how people spread out (or contract) when entering a new space. Confident individuals expand — they put things on the table, adjust the chair, take the space. Anxious individuals contract — arms in, feet close, minimal footprint.

tactics.name: Steepling
tactics.description: Pressing the fingertips of both hands together (like a steeple or praying hands position) — one of the highest-confidence gestures, signalling certainty and authority.
tactics.scoringHints: True steepling: only fingertips touch, palms apart, elbows may rest on surface. Do not confuse with clasped hands (uncertainty) or interlaced fingers (stress). Watch for it in negotiation and interview contexts.

tactics.name: Object Barrier Removal
tactics.description: Moving objects (phones, glasses, folders, cups) that sit between you and the person you're talking to — removing physical barriers improves perceived openness and connection.
tactics.scoringHints: Watch for unconscious barrier placement — phones face-up on the table, arms folded with an object held, a bag placed in the lap. And watch for deliberate removal: pushing a phone aside, moving a folder to clear the space.

tactics.name: Feet Direction Read
tactics.description: Reading the direction of someone's feet as the most reliable indicator of their genuine attention and interest — feet point toward what the mind wants.
tactics.scoringHints: Feet pointed toward the exit signal a desire to leave. Feet pointed toward another person signal interest in them. This applies even when the upper body is rotated toward you — the feet tell the true story.

tactics.name: Controlled Stillness
tactics.description: Minimising physical movement — no swaying, rocking, fidgeting, or unnecessary head movement — to project composure and command attention.
tactics.scoringHints: The stiller the speaker, the more weight their movements carry when they do occur. Watch how actors with controlled stillness make a single gesture more powerful than an animated speaker's dozen gestures.

tactics.name: Proxemic Management
tactics.description: Intentionally managing the distance between yourself and others — using intimate (0–18in), personal (18in–4ft), social (4–12ft), and public (12ft+) zones appropriately.
tactics.scoringHints: Great communicators step closer when sharing something intimate and step back when addressing a group. Watch for when they lean in vs step back — and how the listener responds to each move.

tactics.name: Head Nod Pacing
tactics.description: Using the rate and timing of head nods to pace and influence the listener — slow deliberate nods signal 'I hear you', while rapid nods can be a polite signal to hurry up.
tactics.scoringHints: Three slow nods after someone speaks signals deep listening and encourages them to continue. Rapid nods signal 'wrap it up'. No nods can signal non-agreement or disengagement. The nod rate gives away the listener's patience.

tactics.name: Power Pose Transition
tactics.description: Entering and exiting a space with expansive, upright posture — ensuring that first and last impressions are set by deliberate physical presence rather than anxious contraction.
searchKeywords: power pose transition entry body language
tactics.scoringHints: Watch the first 3 seconds of entry — chest open, gait deliberate, arms slightly away from body. And watch the exit — do they maintain posture as they leave or collapse as they turn away? Both moments set impressions.

tactics.name: Slow Deliberate Entry
tactics.description: Entering a room or conversation at a slightly slower, more deliberate pace than feels natural — claiming the space before speaking.
tactics.scoringHints: Anxious people rush entry — fast walk, eyes down, quick to minimise. Confident people slow down at threshold — pause, scan, then move. The pause before entering is the most overlooked presence technique.

tactics.name: Gravity Walk
tactics.description: A measured, purposeful walking style — chest slightly forward, gait even, no rush — that signals ownership of the space and projects quiet authority.
tactics.scoringHints: The gravity walk is smooth and rhythmic — not military marching. The arms have a natural swing, the torso doesn't bob. The pace is 80–90% of the speaker's natural walking speed. Watch CEOs and actors in casual scenes.

tactics.name: Pre-Talk Stillness
tactics.description: Standing completely still and holding the audience's gaze for 3–5 seconds before uttering a single word — one of the most powerful presence techniques.
searchKeywords: pre talk stillness presence public speaking
tactics.scoringHints: The speaker walks on, establishes their position, and waits. The audience quiets and focuses. When the speaker finally speaks, every word counts because attention was earned by the silence. Watch TED talks for this.

tactics.name: Ownership Gesture
tactics.description: An opening arm sweep or gesture that physically claims the space — unconsciously signalling to the audience that the speaker owns the environment.
searchKeywords: claim the room gesture body language
tactics.scoringHints: A wide outward arm movement at the opening of a speech (like 'welcome to this space') claims the room. More subtle versions: spreading notes on a table, adjusting the microphone stand, or pushing the podium aside.

tactics.name: Full-Body Mirroring
tactics.description: Subtly matching the other person's posture, lean, arm position, and movement rate — creating unconscious rapport through physical synchrony.
tactics.scoringHints: Successful mirroring is invisible — it happens with a 20–30 second delay and partial matching rather than exact copying. Both parties feel unusually comfortable. If mirroring is noticed, it was too obvious.

tactics.name: Cross-Over Mirroring
tactics.description: Mirroring the other person's movements with the opposite limb — right hand to their left hand — which creates rapport without the obvious 'mirror image' quality of direct mirroring.
tactics.scoringHints: Cross-over mirroring is nearly impossible to detect consciously. Watch two people who clearly like each other — you'll see them matching gestures with the opposite limb naturally. It's a sign of genuine synchrony.

tactics.name: Matching Breathing
tactics.description: Syncing your breathing rate and rhythm with your conversation partner — the deepest rapport signal, operating at a largely unconscious level.
tactics.scoringHints: Watch the chest or shoulder movements of two people in deep conversation — when they're fully synchronised, breathing rates match. Therapists and trained negotiators do this deliberately. Almost impossible to fake — it requires genuine attentiveness.

tactics.name: Anchoring
tactics.description: Associating an emotional state with a physical location or touch — so that recreating the physical cue reactivates the emotional state in the future.
tactics.scoringHints: A speaker who always stands in one spot when delivering high-energy content creates an anchor — returning to that spot later reactivates the energy. Handshakes, shoulder touches, and consistent gestures can all function as anchors.

tactics.name: Genuine Smile (Duchenne)
tactics.description: Distinguishing and deploying a genuine smile (Duchenne) — which involves the orbicularis oculi muscle around the eyes — versus a social smile that only uses the mouth.
searchKeywords: genuine vs fake smile body language
tactics.scoringHints: The Duchenne smile: eyes crinkle at the outer corners, the orbicularis oculi contracts. The social smile: only the zygomatic major (lip corners). Watch eyes, not mouth — the mouth is easy to fake; the eyes are not.

tactics.name: Name Mirroring
tactics.description: Using the other person's name at specific, spaced intervals throughout a conversation — creating warmth and personal connection without over-use.
tactics.scoringHints: The name should appear early (to signal you know it), at a key emotional moment, and at closing. Not more than 3 times in a 10-minute conversation — overuse reads as manipulation.

tactics.name: Label and Validate
tactics.description: Naming the emotion or perspective you're observing in the other person ('It seems like you feel strongly about this...') and validating it before responding — a core empathy and negotiation technique.
searchKeywords: name it to tame it communication
tactics.scoringHints: The label is a tentative observation ('It seems like...', 'It sounds like...', 'It looks like...') — not a conclusion. The validation doesn't require agreement ('I can understand why you'd feel that way'). Watch for the visible de-escalation after a correct label.

tactics.name: Minimal Encouragers
tactics.description: Using short, low-effort responses ('I see', 'Go on', 'Tell me more', 'Mmm') to signal attentiveness and encourage the speaker to continue without interrupting their flow.
searchKeywords: go on tell me more listening technique
tactics.scoringHints: These are the invisible glue of good conversations. A listener who never encouragers often creates silences that kill momentum. Watch for how 'mm-hmm' at the right moment makes someone open up further.

tactics.name: Tactical Empathy
tactics.description: Demonstrating genuine understanding of the other person's emotions and perspective — not agreement — to lower defences and build influence through connection.
tactics.scoringHints: Tactical empathy doesn't say 'I agree' — it says 'I understand why you think/feel that'. Watch for the softening that follows: crossed arms unfold, tone becomes less defensive. The moment of empathy is often visible in the listener's physiology.

tactics.name: Calculated Hesitation
tactics.description: Deliberately pausing before responding or deciding — even when you already know the answer — to appear more thoughtful and avoid appearing reactive or impulsive.
tactics.scoringHints: The speaker takes 2–3 seconds before responding to anything — even simple questions. Chin slightly down, gaze briefly unfocused (thinking), then returning to full focus. Signals: 'I consider my words carefully.' Watch negotiators and senior executives.

tactics.name: Narrative Reframe
tactics.description: Re-telling a situation's story from a different point of view or with different emphasis to change how both parties feel about it — the past doesn't change, but its meaning does.
tactics.scoringHints: The speaker accepts the facts as given, then introduces a new interpretation: 'Another way to look at this is...' or 'What this actually shows is...'. Watch for the listener's expression shift from closed to curious as the new story lands.

tactics.name: The Power of 'And'
tactics.description: Replacing 'but' with 'and' when responding to disagreement — which acknowledges both perspectives rather than invalidating the first with the second.
tactics.scoringHints: 'I hear you, BUT...' cancels everything before the 'but.' 'I hear you, AND here's another perspective...' holds both. Listen for whether speakers use 'but' or 'and' when pivoting — it reveals how they're processing disagreement.

tactics.name: Strategic Self-Disclosure
tactics.description: Sharing calibrated personal information at the right moment to accelerate trust — the disclosure signals you're safe enough to share, and invites reciprocal openness.
tactics.scoringHints: The disclosure is personal enough to be meaningful but not so heavy it creates discomfort. 'I've made this mistake too...' is strategic. It's calibrated to the relationship depth and context. Watch for how reciprocal disclosure follows.

tactics.name: Controlled Eye Break
tactics.description: Deliberately breaking eye contact downward (not sideways) when processing a question or accessing a memory — a universal signal of internal thought rather than evasion.
tactics.scoringHints: Eyes break down + slight chin tuck = accessing memory or thought. Eyes break sideways = evasion (in most cultures). The key is returning quickly to full eye contact after processing. A brief, clean eye break is far better than maintaining uncomfortable stare while thinking.

tactics.name: Gestural Emphasis
tactics.description: Timing hand gestures to land precisely on the key word of a sentence — so that the gesture and the important word arrive at the same moment, doubling the impact.
tactics.scoringHints: A gesture that arrives before or after the key word is desynchronised. A perfectly timed gesture and word feel like a physical punch. Watch politicians and trial lawyers — they rehearse this synchrony until it becomes natural.
